transcript:
[doctor] well good morning emily how are you
[patient] good morning
[doctor] how are you doing
[patient] doing okay i'm okay
[doctor] i understand you're having some sudden visual distortions and some blurring of your vision is that right
[patient] it is yes
[doctor] okay so what's going on
[patient] sorry to put you off i do n't think i have that script
[doctor] oh
[patient] maybe a different one i wonder if patient has history of
[doctor] macular degeneration yeah yeah i was because we do n't we do n't to do the intro anymore i was just going with that you you're coming in with sudden visual distortion and blurring of vision so i'll just you know i'll just say i'll just go ahead and go into this technician part so it looks like the technician noticed that you've been having a decrease in vision when reading and straight lines and your door frames have been looking crooked to you is that right
[patient] it is yes
[doctor] okay what kind of venture are we gon na go into do you do you have the the script or no it it just talks
[patient] but it's it's not presenting like like how you're reading it like how you're talking about it is it kinda what i'm saying because like after that i'm supposed to say yeah whenever i take my husband out to like a nice restaurant i have trouble adjusting to those dim lights but i'm not really getting the question of like so what's causing it or where do you have this happening stuff like that so i'm a little confused
[doctor] okay i mean i can just straight out follow the script just so we can kinda get it rolling so so i said so hi so it looks like the technician noticed that you've been having a decrease in vision when reading and straight lines in your door frames have been looking crooked to you
[patient] yeah and whenever i take my husband out to a nice restaurant i have trouble adjusting to those dim lights
[doctor] uh okay so do you have a favorite restaurant you like to go to
[patient] i do mcdonalds is one of my favorite here in arlington
[doctor] that sounds good what what do you what's your favorite there
[patient] they have a great egg plant parmajon one of my favorite so i just go ahead and go with that
[doctor] that sounds really nice very good
[patient] it's just
[doctor] yeah okay well so it looks like you're still smoking a pack of cigarettes a day
[patient] yes i tried to stop smoking every now and then but it's still been hard to totally stop
[doctor] okay and how is your blood pressure been doing are you keeping up with your medication
[patient] i take my blood pressure when i can remember to do so
[doctor] okay yeah it's really important to keep up with your blood pressure check that regularly i'm totally going off script and you know might want to keep a log of that as well and check your blood pressures regularly so okay well let me do a just a quick exam of your eyes if you can take off your glasses and just rest your chin on the chin rest okay so the anterior segment examination reveals posterior chamber intraocular lenses in both eyes right eye the cornea is clear no mvi pciol cup to disc is . two fundus examination of the right eye shows the retina to be attached three hundred and sixty degrees without any tears or holes or pe changes in the fovea posterior segment exam shows drusen in the macula thickening of the retina presence of subretinal fluid and exudates accumulation positive for hemorrhage left eye the cornea is clear no mvi cup to disc is . three or pe changes in the fovea no hemorrhage or subretinal fluid retina is attached okay so i reviewed the results of your diagnostic tests so for the oct so it shows drusen deposit and the retinal pigment epithelium subretinal fluid accumulation choroidal neovascular membrane can be visualized fluorescein angiography reveals focal areas of leakage in the right eye consistent with neurovascular age related macular degeneration so the diagnostic tests and the eye exam are really indicative of neovascular age related macular degeneration so this is advanced to a stage that usually leads to more severe vision loss and it really happens when those abnormal blood vessels start to grow beneath the retina and they will leak fluid and blood and hints the name wet amd and can create a large blind spot in the center of the visual field and this is really with the most common cause for severe loss of vision so my plan is to give you an injection of lucentes once a month it's injected into the vitreous portion of the eye after it's been numbed and injections are given regularly over a period of time it may slow the rate of vision decline or stop further vision loss but however the disease and loss it may also progress despite treatment do you have any questions
[patient] i do not no that sounds great
[doctor] okay
gold_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Changes in vision.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Patient smokes approximately 1 pack of cigarettes per day.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Eyes: Reports visual distortions and blurred vision.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Eyes
- Examination: Anterior segment examination reveals posterior chamber intraocular lenses in both eyes.
Right eye: The cornea is clear. No MVI. PCIOL cup to disc is 0.2. Fundus examination of the right eye shows the retina to be attached 360 degrees without any tears or holes. RPE changes in the fovea. Posterior segment exam shows drusen in the macula, thickening of the retina, presence of subretinal fluid and exudates accumulation. Positive for hemorrhage.
Left eye: The cornea is clear. No MVI. Cup to disc is 0.3. RPE changes in the fovea. No hemorrhage or subretinal fluid. Retina is attached.

RESULTS

Optical coherence tomography shows drusen deposit. Retinal pigment epithelium with subretinal fluid accumulation. The choroidal neovascular membrane can be visualized. Fluorescein angiography reveals focal areas of leakage in the right eye consistent with neovascular age-related macular degeneration.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Neovascular age-related macular degeneration.
- Medical Reasoning: Based on her diagnostic testing and eye exam, this is at an advanced stage.
- Patient Education and Counseling: We discussed the nature of the condition, as well as the recommendation for Lucentis injections. I explained the procedure in detail and advised the patient of the potential outcomes.
- Medical Treatment: We will proceed with a once-monthly Lucenti's injections.

Patient Agreements: The patient understands and agrees with the recommended medical treatment plan.
notes:
Subjective:
HPI: The patient presents with sudden visual distortions and blurring of vision. She reports that straight lines and door frames appear crooked to her and she has trouble adjusting to dim lights, particularly in restaurants. She has a history of macular degeneration and is a smoker, consuming a pack of cigarettes per day. She reports difficulty stopping smoking and infrequently checks her blood pressure.

Past medical history: The patient has a history of macular degeneration.

Review of systems: The patient reports difficulty adjusting to dim lights in restaurants.

Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]

Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]

Physical exam: The anterior segment examination reveals posterior chamber intraocular lenses in both eyes, with clear corneas. The cup-to-disc ratio is 0.2 in the right eye and 0.3 in the left eye. The fundus examination shows the retina to be attached 360 degrees without tears or holes in the right eye, and the posterior segment exam reveals drusen in the macula, thickening of the retina, presence of subretinal fluid, and exudates accumulation positive for hemorrhage in the left eye.

Test Results: The OCT shows drusen deposit, subretinal fluid accumulation, choroidal neovascular membrane, and focal areas of leakage in the right eye consistent with neovascular age-related macular degeneration.

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has neovascular age-related macular degeneration, an advanced stage that typically leads to severe vision loss due to the growth of abnormal blood vessels beneath the retina, leaking fluid and blood and creating a large blind spot in the center of the visual field.

Problem list: Age-related macular degeneration; active.

Plan: I prescribed Lucentis injections once a month, injected into the vitreous portion of the eye after numbing, to be given regularly over a period of time to slow the rate of vision decline or stop further vision loss. The patient should continue to monitor their blood pressure regularly and consider keeping a log of their readings.

Follow-up: I will see the patient again to monitor the effectiveness of the treatment and adjust the plan as needed.

Subjective:
The patient presents with sudden visual distortions and blurring of vision. She reports difficulty adjusting to dim lights in restaurants and has noticed that straight lines appear crooked to her. She has a history of macular degeneration and is a smoker, consuming a pack of cigarettes per day. She has not been consistent with her blood pressure medication, only taking it when she remembers. She has a favorite restaurant, McDonald's, where she enjoys the eggplant parmesan.

Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The anterior segment examination reveals posterior chamber intraocular lenses in both eyes. The right eye's cornea is clear, with no MVI, and the cup-to-disc ratio is 0.2. The fundus examination of the right eye shows the retina to be attached 360 degrees without any tears, holes, or pe changes in the fovea. The posterior segment exam shows drusen in the macula, thickening of the retina, presence of subretinal fluid, and exudates, with a positive hemorrhage. The left eye's cornea is clear, with no MVI, and the cup-to-disc ratio is 0.3, with no pe changes in the fovea, no hemorrhage, and the retina is attached.

Assessment / Problem List:
The patient has neovascular age-related macular degeneration, which is an advanced stage of the disease that can lead to severe vision loss.

Problem list: Age-related macular degeneration; suspected severe vision loss; smoking; hypertension; inconsistent blood pressure medication use.

Plan:
I prescribed Lucentis injections once a month, injected into the vitreous portion of the eye, after numbing, to slow the rate of vision decline or stop further vision loss. However, the disease may progress despite treatment.

Follow-up: [NOT MENTIONED]

Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Emily, presents with sudden visual distortions and blurring of vision. She reports that straight lines and door frames appear crooked to her, and she has difficulty adjusting to dim lights, particularly in restaurants. She mentions that she has had trouble with this issue even when taking her husband out to nice restaurants, such as McDonald's in Arlington. She also reports a history of smoking a pack of cigarettes a day, despite attempting to quit. Her blood pressure has been inconsistent, and she has not been regularly checking it.

Past medical history: The patient has a history of macular degeneration. She has not provided further information about her medical history.

Review of systems: The patient reports no other symptoms.

Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]

Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]

Physical exam: The anterior segment examination reveals posterior chamber intraocular lenses in both eyes. The right eye's cornea is clear, with no MVI. The cup-to-disc ratio is 0.2. The fundus examination of the right eye shows the retina to be attached 360 degrees without tears, holes, or pe changes in the fovea. The posterior segment exam shows drusen in the macula, thickening of the retina, presence of subretinal fluid, and exudates accumulation positive for hemorrhage. The left eye's cornea is clear, with no MVI. The cup-to-disc ratio is 0.3. The retina is attached.

Test Results: The OCT shows drusen deposit and the retinal pigment epithelium, subretinal fluid accumulation, and choroidal neovascular membrane. Fluorescein angiography reveals focal areas of leakage in the right eye consistent with neovascular age-related macular degeneration.

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has neovascular age-related macular degeneration, an advanced stage that can lead to severe vision loss.

Problem list: Neovascular age-related macular degeneration; active.

Plan:
I prescribed Lucentis 0.5 mg, administered via intravitreal injection, to be given monthly for the treatment of neovascular age-related macular degeneration. I instructed the patient to keep a log of her blood pressure and to check it regularly.

Follow-up: I scheduled a follow-up appointment for the patient to review the effectiveness of the treatment and to adjust the treatment plan as necessary.

Subjective:
HPI: The patient presents with sudden visual distortions and blurring of vision, which began suddenly. The patient reports that straight lines and door frames appear crooked to them, and they have trouble adjusting to dim lights, particularly in restaurants. The patient mentions that they have a history of macular degeneration.

Past medical history: The patient has a history of macular degeneration, but no other relevant past medical conditions are mentioned. The patient currently smokes one pack of cigarettes per day, but has tried to quit on several occasions.

Review of systems: The patient reports trouble adjusting to dim lights in restaurants.

Current medications: NOT MENTIONED

Objective:
Vital signs: NOT MENTIONED

Physical exam: The anterior segment examination reveals posterior chamber intraocular lenses in both eyes, with the right eye's cornea being clear and no mvi. The cup to disc ratio is 0.2. The fundus examination of the right eye shows the retina to be attached and without any tears, holes, or pe changes in the fovea. The posterior segment exam reveals drusen in the macula, thickening of the retina, presence of subretinal fluid, and exudates accumulation with positive hemorrhage in the right eye. The left eye's cornea is clear, with no mvi, and the cup to disc ratio is 0.3. The retina is attached, without any hemorrhage or subretinal fluid.

Test Results: The OCT shows drusen deposit and retinal pigment epithelium subretinal fluid accumulation, choroidal neovascular membrane, and focal areas of leakage in the right eye consistent with neovascular age-related macular degeneration.

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has advanced neovascular age-related macular degeneration with focal areas of leakage in the right eye.

Problem list: Age-related macular degeneration; active; Neovascular age-related macular degeneration; active.

Plan:
I plan to give the patient an injection of Lucentis once a month, injected into the vitreous portion of the eye after it has been numbed. Injections will be given regularly over a period of time, which may slow the rate of vision decline or stop further vision loss. However, the disease and loss may progress despite treatment.

Follow-up: I will see the patient again for follow-up appointments to monitor the progression of the disease and adjust treatment as necessary.

Subjective:

The patient reports difficulty adjusting to dim lights, specifically when going out to restaurants, and notes that straight lines and door frames appear crooked. The patient has a history of macular degeneration and is a smoker, consuming one pack of cigarettes per day.

Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]

Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]

Physical exam: The anterior segment examination revealed posterior chamber intraocular lenses in both eyes, clear cornea, and normal cup to disc ratio in both eyes. The fundus examination of the right eye showed a retina attached with no tears or holes, but with changes in the fovea. The posterior segment examination revealed drusen in the macula, thickening of the retina, presence of subretinal fluid, and exudates accumulation, and positive for hemorrhage in the right eye. The left eye had a clear cornea, normal cup to disc ratio, and no changes in the fovea, with no hemorrhage or subretinal fluid, and an attached retina.

Test Results: The OCT shows drusen deposit and retinal pigment epithelium subretinal fluid accumulation, choroidal neovascular membrane, and focal areas of leakage in the right eye consistent with neovascular age-related macular degeneration.

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has advanced neovascular age-related macular degeneration.

Problem list: Age-related macular degeneration, active.

Plan:
I prescribed Lucentis once a month for the treatment of neovascular age-related macular degeneration. The injection will be administered into the vitreous portion of the eye after numbing, and injections will be given regularly over a period of time to slow the rate of vision decline or stop further vision loss. The patient will be instructed to keep a log of their blood pressure checks and to check their blood pressure regularly.

Follow-up: The patient will be followed up with me in the future to monitor the progression of the disease and adjust treatment as needed.
